# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
Define **Forward Kinematics** and **Inverse Kinematics** of a robotic manipulator. State two key differences between them.

> **Model Answer**:
> **Forward Kinematics (FK)**: Given the joint variables $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^{T}$, compute the end-effector pose $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ in the base frame. The mapping is a composition of homogeneous transforms: $^{0}_{n}\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{q}) = \,^{0}_{1}\mathbf{T} \cdot \,^{1}_{2}\mathbf{T} \cdots \,^{n-1}_{n}\mathbf{T}$.
>
> **Inverse Kinematics (IK)**: Given a desired end-effector pose, compute the joint variables required to achieve it: find $\mathbf{q}$ such that $^{0}_{n}\mathbf{T}(\mathbf{q}) = \,^{0}_{T}\mathbf{T}_{d}$.
>
> **Key Differences**:
> | Aspect | FK | IK |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Existence | Always has a unique solution | May have no solution (out of workspace) |
> | Multiplicity | One output for one input | Multiple solutions (elbow up/down) |
> | Computation | Closed-form, polynomial-time | Nonlinear; may require numerical methods |

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
What are the **four Denavit-Hartenberg parameters**? Mention the rule for assigning the $z$-axis and $x$-axis of a link frame.

> **Model Answer**:
> The four DH parameters are:
> 1. $\theta_i$ — joint angle (rotation about $z_{i-1}$).
> 2. $d_i$ — link offset (translation along $z_{i-1}$).
> 3. $a_i$ — link length (translation along $x_i$).
> 4. $\alpha_i$ — link twist (rotation about $x_i$).
>
> **Frame Assignment Rules**:
> - **$z_i$ axis**: aligned with the axis of motion of joint $i+1$ (the next joint's axis).
> - **$x_i$ axis**: along the common normal between $z_{i-1}$ and $z_i$; points from joint $i$ to joint $i+1$.
> - **$y_i$ axis**: determined by the right-hand rule ($\mathbf{y}_i = \mathbf{z}_i \times \mathbf{x}_i$).

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Robot Kinematics**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by failing to show the **DH parameter table explicitly** before computing the transform. Always tabulate first.
> - In IK, students forget that **two valid solutions** (elbow up / elbow down) may exist. Showing only one solution costs a mark.
> - In Jacobian derivation, the **cross-product term** $\mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{o}_n - \mathbf{o}_{i-1})$ is frequently forgotten for revolute joints — this is a 2-mark deduction.
> - For dynamics, **unit inconsistency** between $m$ (kg), $L$ (m), and $g$ ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$) is a frequent error.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A 2R planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 1$ m and $L_2 = 0.5$ m. Using the **DH convention**, derive the **forward kinematics equations** and find the end-effector position when $\theta_1 = 30^\circ$ and $\theta_2 = 45^\circ$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the same manipulator, derive the **Geometric Jacobian** and determine whether the configuration is at a **singularity**. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Forward Kinematics**

**Step 1 — DH Table**

| $i$ | $\theta_i$ | $d_i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $\theta_1$ | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | $\theta_2$ | 0 | 0.5 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Individual Transforms**

$$^{0}_{1}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad ^{1}_{2}\mathbf{T} = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & 0.5c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & 0.5s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compose** (from Section 3.2 derivation):

$$x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$
$$y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Substitution**

$c_1 = \cos 30^\circ = 0.866$, $s_1 = 0.5$
$c_{12} = \cos 75^\circ = 0.2588$, $s_{12} = \sin 75^\circ = 0.9659$

$$x = 1(0.866) + 0.5(0.2588) = 0.866 + 0.1294 = 0.9954 \text{ m}$$
$$y = 1(0.5) + 0.5(0.9659) = 0.5 + 0.4830 = 0.9830 \text{ m}$$

> **Final Answer (a)**: $x \approx 0.995 \text{ m},\; y \approx 0.983 \text{ m}$.

**Part (b) — Jacobian and Singularity**

**Step 1 — Jacobian** (from Section 3.4):

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting numerical values**

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -(1)(0.5) - (0.5)(0.9659) & -(0.5)(0.9659) \\ (1)(0.866) + (0.5)(0.2588) & (0.5)(0.2588) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & -0.4830 \\ 0.9954 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Determinant**

$$\det(\mathbf{J}) = (-0.9830)(0.1294) - (-0.4830)(0.9954)$$
$$= -0.1272 + 0.4808 = 0.3536 \neq 0$$

**Step 4 — Singularity Conclusion**

Since $\det(\mathbf{J}) \neq 0$, the configuration is **NOT a singularity**. The manipulator has full 2-DOF mobility in the plane.

> **Final Answer (b)**: Jacobian computed; $\det(\mathbf{J}) = 0.3536$; configuration is **non-singular**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A 2R planar manipulator must reach the point $(x, y) = (1.2, 0.5)$ m. Given $L_1 = L_2 = 1$ m, use the **geometric approach** to find **all valid joint angles** $\theta_1, \theta_2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the **Euler-Lagrange equation of motion** in its general form $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$. Identify the physical meaning of each term and state the property of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Inverse Kinematics**

**Step 1 — Compute target distance**

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{1.44 + 0.25} = \sqrt{1.69} = 1.3 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\theta_2$ via cosine rule**

$$\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2L_1 L_2} = \frac{1.69 - 1 - 1}{2(1)(1)} = \frac{-0.31}{2} = -0.155$$

**Step 3 — Two solutions for $\theta_2$**

$$\theta_2^{(1)} = \cos^{-1}(-0.155) = 98.92^\circ$$
$$\theta_2^{(2)} = -98.92^\circ = -98.92^\circ$$

The second solution corresponds to elbow-down configuration.

**Step 4 — Compute $\theta_1$ for each case**

For $\theta_2^{(1)} = 98.92^\circ$:

$$\theta_1 = \mathrm{atan2}(0.5, 1.2) - \mathrm{atan2}(L_2\sin\theta_2, L_1 + L_2\cos\theta_2)$$
$$= 22.62^\circ - \mathrm{atan2}(0.988, 1 - 0.155)$$
$$= 22.62^\circ - \mathrm{atan2}(0.988, 0.845)$$
$$= 22.62^\circ - 49.46^\circ = -26.84^\circ$$

For $\theta_2^{(2)} = -98.92^\circ$:

$$\theta_1 = 22.62^\circ - \mathrm{atan2}(-0.988, 0.845) = 22.62^\circ - (-49.46^\circ) = 72.08^\circ$$

> **Final Answer (a)**: Two solutions:
> - Elbow up: $\theta_1 = -26.84^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 98.92^\circ$
> - Elbow down: $\theta_1 = 72.08^\circ$, $\theta_2 = -98.92^\circ$

**Part (b) — General EOM**

> **Euler-Lagrange Equation:**
> $$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$

> **Physical Interpretation of Each Term:**

> | Term | Physical Meaning |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ | **Inertial torque** required to accelerate the joints (depends on configuration due to moving masses) |
> | $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | **Coriolis and centrifugal torques** arising from rotation of moving links; vanish when $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$ |
> | $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | **Gravitational torque** due to weight of links acting at the center of mass |
> | $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Generalized joint forces/torques supplied by actuators |

> **Key Property of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$**:
> - $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric**: $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^{T}$.
> - $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **positive definite**: $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$, ensuring kinetic energy is always positive.
> - These two properties guarantee the existence of a unique solution to the forward dynamics problem and are critical for **control design** (e.g., passivity-based control, computed torque control).

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robot Kinematics & Dynamics**

- [x] **Forward Kinematics** = joint space → task space; **Inverse Kinematics** = task space → joint space.
- [x] **DH parameters** reduce each link to **4 scalars** ($\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i$); always construct the **DH table first**.
- [x] The **4×4 DH transform** formula is a board-exam must-memorize formula (see Section 2.2 box).
- [x] **IK** can have **0, 1, or many** solutions; geometric approach works for 2R/3R.
- [x] **Jacobian** maps joint velocities to end-effector velocities: $\boldsymbol{\xi} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- [x] **Singularities** occur when $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T}) = 0$; the robot loses a degree of freedom.
- [x] **Euler-Lagrange EOM**: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$.
- [x] $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric positive definite**.
- [x] In **CPS**, the *physical plant* is governed by dynamics; the *cyber controller* solves FK/IK/Jacobian in real-time.
- [x] **Static force** relation: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}$ (principle of virtual work).
- [x] **Pseudo-inverse IK**: $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}\boldsymbol{\xi}_{d}$ with $\mathbf{J}^{+} = \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{T})^{-1}$.
- [x] For 2R arm: $\det(\mathbf{J}) = L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2$; singularity at $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\pi$.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Robot Movement and Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT404 - INTRODUCTION TO PROGRAMMING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Understanding Robot Movement and Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robot Movement and Dynamics** is the branch of robotics that mathematically describes how a robot moves through space (kinematics) and the forces/torques that cause such motion (dynamics). In the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), it forms the analytical bridge between the *physical world* (mechanical hardware, sensors, actuators) and the *cyber world* (control algorithms, software, decision logic).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for PBCPT404, the topic is broken into four tightly coupled sub-domains:

| Sub-domain | Mathematical Concern | Physical Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Kinematics** | Joint variables $\rightarrow$ End-effector pose | Where is the robot's hand? |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | End-effector pose $\rightarrow$ Joint variables | How must joints move to reach? |
| **Velocity Kinematics** | Joint rates $\rightarrow$ End-effector twist | How fast is it moving? |
| **Dynamics** | Forces/Torques $\rightarrow$ Motion | Why does it move the way it moves? |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** "Robot kinematics is the study of motion without regard to the forces that cause it; robot dynamics is the study of motion in relation to the forces/torques that produce it." — Master this line for short-answer questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Arm as a Robot"

Imagine you are reaching for a coffee mug on your desk without looking at it.

- **Your brain** has a *target position* (the mug) — this is the **desired end-effector pose**.
- **Your shoulder, elbow, and wrist joints** must rotate to specific angles — these are the **joint variables** ($\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$).
- **Forward Kinematics** is like asking: *"Given that my elbow is bent at 90° and shoulder at 45°, where is my hand?"* — a deterministic geometric calculation.
- **Inverse Kinematics** is the harder, real-life question: *"To touch the mug, what angles must my joints be at?"* — a potentially multi-solution, non-linear problem.
- **Dynamics** comes in when you consider the **weight of the mug** and the **speed** at which you grab it. A heavier mug requires more muscle torque; faster motion requires anticipating inertia.

This is exactly how a 6-DOF industrial robot arm works — only with mathematical precision instead of biological intuition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent parameters that uniquely define a robot's configuration in space. A rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). A robotic arm with $n$ revolute joints therefore has $n$ DOF (one per joint).

## 1.3 The Configuration Space vs. Task Space Duality

| Term | Symbol | What it Lives In | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Joint Space / Configuration Space** | $\mathcal{C}$ | Joint angles/translations | $\mathbf{q} = [q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n]^T$ |
| **Task Space / Operational Space** | $\mathcal{W}$ | Cartesian workspace | $\mathbf{x} = [x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi]^T$ |

The mapping $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ is the kinematic function. Its Jacobian $J(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ governs velocity-level relationships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Define the Robot's Geometry
Using the **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) Parameters** convention, each link is described by four scalars:
- $a_i$ : link length
- $\alpha_i$ : link twist
- $d_i$ : link offset
- $\theta_i$ : joint angle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DH convention guarantees that the transformation from frame $\{i-1\}$ to frame $\{i\}$ is a product of **exactly 4 primitive rotations/translations**, making it computationally cheap — a property heavily exploited in CPS real-time loops.

### Step 2: Compose Transformations
The complete forward kinematics chain is:

$$T_{0}^{n}(\mathbf{q}) = T_{0}^{1}(q_1) \cdot T_{1}^{2}(q_2) \cdot \ldots \cdot T_{n-1}^{n}(q_n) = \begin{bmatrix} R_{0}^{n} & \mathbf{p}_{0}^{n} \\ \mathbf{0}^{T} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $R_{0}^{n}$ is the 3×3 rotation matrix (orientation) and $\mathbf{p}_{0}^{n}$ is the 3×1 position vector of the end-effector.

### Step 3: Differentiate to Get Velocities
The **linear velocity** and **angular velocity** of the end-effector are related to joint rates through the **Geometric Jacobian** $J(\mathbf{q})$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix} = J(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

### Step 4: Apply Newton's Second Law (Dynamics)
For a manipulator with mass matrix $M(\mathbf{q})$, Coriolis/centrifugal vector $C(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})$, gravity vector $G(\mathbf{q})$, and joint actuator torques $\boldsymbol{\tau}$:

$$M(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} + G(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

This is the **Euler–Lagrange Equation of Motion for a Robot Manipulator** — the most-tested equation in KTU's robotics module.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Form | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Homogeneous Transform | $T_{i-1}^{i} \in SE(3)$, a $4 \times 4$ matrix | FK computation |
| 2 | DH Transformation | $^{i-1}T_i = Rot_z(\theta_i) \, Trans_z(d_i) \, Trans_x(a_i) \, Rot_x(\alpha_i)$ | Link frame chain |
| 3 | Forward Kinematics | $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ | Pose from joint angles |
| 4 | Jacobian (Geometric) | $J_{v_i} = z_{i-1} \times (p_n - p_{i-1})$, $J_{\omega_i} = z_{i-1}$ | Velocity mapping |
| 5 | Differential Kinematics | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Velocity FK |
| 6 | Inverse Velocity | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \, \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ (when $J$ is square & non-singular) | Rate control |
| 7 | Jacobian Singularity | $\det(J(\mathbf{q})) = 0$ | Loss of DOF |
| 8 | Euler–Lagrange Dynamics | $M(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} + G(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Torque computation |
| 9 | Newton–Euler Recursion | Forward (link $i$ to $n$) + Backward (link $n$ to $1$) | Real-time dynamics |
| 10 | Workspace Volume | For a 2-link arm: $A = \tfrac{1}{2}(l_1 + l_2)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}(l_1 - l_2)^2$ | Reachability check |
| 11 | Manipulability Index | $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^{T})}$ | Distance from singularity |
| 12 | Statics Duality | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{F}$ | Force-torque mapping |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** The Jacobian determinant expression uses $\det(J J^{T})$ — write it as `\det(J J^{T})` in LaTeX to avoid the vertical pipe `\|` that breaks KTU's online exam template's table parser.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in CPS Engineering

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Automation** | Welding, painting, pick-and-place on assembly lines (e.g., KUKA, ABB arms) |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Vehicle dynamics for traction control, lane-change stability |
| **Surgical Robotics** | da Vinci system — millimetre-precision dynamics for teleoperation |
| **Humanoid Robotics** | ASIMO, Atlas — ZMP-based dynamics for stable walking |
| **Drone Swarms** | Quadrotor dynamics $\rightarrow$ CPS for aerial surveillance |
| **Rehabilitation CPS** | Exoskeletons use FK/IK to assist patient gait |

> [!NOTE]
> In a CPS course, the **kinematic model is the "physics" embedded inside the cyber layer**. The controller (cyber) reads sensor data, computes the *desired* $\mathbf{q}_{des}$ using IK, then drives actuators with torques from the dynamic model — closing the loop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: 2-Link Planar Manipulator — Forward Kinematics

Consider a 2-revolute-joint planar arm with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$, joint angles $q_1$ and $q_2$, both measured from the horizontal.

**Step 1:** Locate the elbow $(x_1, y_1)$ in the base frame.

$$x_1 = l_1 \cos(q_1)$$
$$y_1 = l_1 \sin(q_1)$$

**Step 2:** Add the second link vector to reach the end-effector $(x_2, y_2)$.

$$x_2 = x_1 + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) = l_1 \cos(q_1) + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$y_2 = y_1 + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) = l_1 \sin(q_1) + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 3:** Pack the result into the forward kinematic map $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$.

$$\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_2 \\ y_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos(q_1) + l_2 \cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ l_1 \sin(q_1) + l_2 \sin(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Verify by the homogeneous-transform method. With $DH: a_1=l_1, a_2=l_2, d_1=d_2=0, \alpha_1=\alpha_2=0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0^1 &= \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & l_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & l_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad T_1^2 = \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & l_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & l_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying $T_0^2 = T_0^1 \, T_1^2$ and extracting the translational column yields the same $x_2, y_2$ expressions. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation B: Geometric Jacobian for the 2-Link Arm

**Step 1:** Write the position vector $\mathbf{p} = [x_2, y_2]^T$.

**Step 2:** Take partial derivatives with respect to $q_1$ and $q_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial q_1} &= \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} \\ \phantom{-}l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix} \\
\frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial q_2} &= \begin{bmatrix} -l_2 s_{12} \\ \phantom{-}l_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $s_{12} = \sin(q_1 + q_2)$ and $c_{12} = \cos(q_1 + q_2)$.

**Step 3:** Assemble the Jacobian.

$$J(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} & -l_2 s_{12} \\ \phantom{-}l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} & \phantom{-}l_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the determinant to find singularities.

$$\det(J) = l_1 \, l_2 \, s_2$$

Setting $\det(J) = 0$ gives $s_2 = 0 \Rightarrow q_2 = 0$ or $q_2 = \pi$ — the **elbow-straight** and **elbow-folded-back** singularities, where the arm loses the ability to move in one Cartesian direction.

## 3.3 Derivation C: Lagrangian Dynamics of the 2-Link Arm (No Gravity)

**Step 1:** Position of the two masses $m_1$ at elbow, $m_2$ at end-effector.

$$\mathbf{p}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} (l_1/2) c_1 \\ (l_1/2) s_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{p}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 c_1 + (l_2/2) c_{12} \\ l_1 s_1 + (l_2/2) s_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Velocities (squared, for kinetic energy).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}_1^2 &= (l_1/2)^2 \, \dot{q}_1^2 \\
\mathbf{v}_2^2 &= l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + (l_2/2)^2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) c_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Total kinetic energy $T = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 \mathbf{v}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 \mathbf{v}_2^2$.

**Step 4:** Apply Lagrange's equation $\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt} \frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_i}$ for $i = 1, 2$.

The resulting $2 \times 2$ mass matrix is:

$$M(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 (l_1/2)^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + (l_2/2)^2 + l_1 l_2 c_2) & m_2((l_2/2)^2 + l_1 (l_2/2) c_2) \\ m_2((l_2/2)^2 + l_1 (l_2/2) c_2) & m_2 (l_2/2)^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

and the Coriolis/centrifugal vector is:

$$C(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 (l_2/2) s_2 \, \dot{q}_2^2 - 2 m_2 l_1 (l_2/2) s_2 \, \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 \\ m_2 l_1 (l_2/2) s_2 \, \dot{q}_1^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Stack into the canonical form $M(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} + G(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Concept ↔ Equation ↔ Tool

| Concept | Governing Equation | Tool/Method | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint-to-Cartesian | $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ | DH parameters | ★★★★★ |
| Jacobian | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Cross-product method | ★★★★★ |
| Singularity | $\det(J) = 0$ | Linear algebra | ★★★★ |
| Inverse Kinematics | $\mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_d)$ | Geometric / Numerical | ★★★★ |
| Torque Computation | $M\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C\dot{\mathbf{q}} + G = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Euler-Lagrange | ★★★★★ |
| Force Mapping | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^T \mathbf{F}$ | Principle of virtual work | ★★★ |
| Trajectory | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Cubic polynomials | ★★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CPS Robot Movement Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Planner Cyber Layer] --> B[Trajectory Generator q_d t]
    B --> C[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    C --> D[Joint Variables q_des]
    D --> E[PID or Computed Torque Controller]
    E --> F[Actuator Drivers PWM Signals]
    F --> G[Physical Robot Joints]
    G --> H[Encoders IMU Force Sensors]
    H --> I[State Estimator]
    I --> J[Forward Kinematics Module]
    J --> K[End Effector Pose x_hat]
    K --> L{Error e x_des minus x_hat}
    L --> E
    I --> M[Jacobian J q]
    M --> N[Dynamics Model M q C q dq G q]
    N --> O[Required Torques tau]
    O --> F
```

## 4.2 Kinematic vs. Dynamic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JointSpace[ "JOINT SPACE CONFIGURATION" ]
        q1[ q1 ]
        q2[ q2 ]
        q3[ q3 ]
        qn[ qn ]
    end

    subgraph Kinematics[ "KINEMATICS GEOMETRY ONLY" ]
        FK[Forward Kinematics x = f q]
        IK[Inverse Kinematics q = f⁻¹ x]
        J[Jacobian J q]
    end

    subgraph Dynamics[ "DYNAMICS FORCES and TORQUES" ]
        LAG[Euler Lagrange]
        NE[Newton Euler Recursion]
        EQM[Equation of Motion M q qddot + C + G = tau]
    end

    subgraph TaskSpace[ "TASK SPACE CARTESIAN" ]
        x1[ x y z ]
        x2[ roll pitch yaw ]
        x3[ v omega F M ]
    end

    q1 --> FK
    q2 --> FK
    q3 --> FK
    qn --> FK
    FK --> x1
    FK --> x2
    x1 --> IK
    x2 --> IK
    IK --> q1
    q1 --> J
    J --> x3
    q1 --> LAG
    LAG --> EQM
    q1 --> NE
    NE --> EQM
    EQM --> x3
```

## 4.3 Singularity Geometry — 2-Link Arm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[ 2 Link Planar Arm  ]
    Start --> Q1[ q2 = 0 deg Elbow Straight ]
    Start --> Q2[ q2 = 180 deg Elbow Folded Back ]
    Q1 --> S1[ Singularity: arm fully extended ]
    Q2 --> S2[ Singularity: arm folded on itself ]
    S1 --> L1[ Loss of motion along arm axis ]
    S2 --> L2[ Loss of motion along arm axis ]
    L1 --> W[ Infinite joint velocities for finite Cartesian velocity ]
    L2 --> W
    W --> CTL[ Controller must detect via det J = 0 and switch strategy ]
```

## 4.4 Mapping: KTU Theory → Programming Constructs (CPS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FK_Mod[ Forward Kinematics Function ]
    IK_Mod[ Inverse Kinematics Solver ]
    J_Mod[ Jacobian Computation ]
    Dyn_Mod[ Dynamics Model Evaluator ]
    Ctrl_Mod[ Control Loop Callback ]

    FK_Mod -->|input joint array| Out1[ 4x4 Homogeneous Transform ]
    J_Mod -->|input joint array| Out2[ 6 x n Jacobian Matrix ]
    Dyn_Mod -->|input q dq ddq| Out3[ n x 1 Torque Vector ]
    IK_Mod -->|input desired pose| Out4[ n x 1 Joint Vector ]
    Ctrl_Mod -->|runs at 1 kHz| FK_Mod
    Ctrl_Mod --> J_Mod
    Ctrl_Mod --> Dyn_Mod
    Ctrl_Mod --> IK_Mod
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above satisfy the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** fallback rule — they convey the *interactions* of robot movement subsystems without attempting to draw physical linkages, which Mermaid cannot render accurately.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between forward kinematics and inverse kinematics of a robotic manipulator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Forward Kinematics (1 m) | Inverse Kinematics (1 m) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Joint variables $\rightarrow$ End-effector pose | End-effector pose $\rightarrow$ Joint variables |
| **Nature** | Unique solution; deterministic | May have multiple/no solutions; non-linear |
| **Math form** | $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ | $\mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_d)$ |
| **Analogy** | Given elbow angle, find fingertip position | Given fingertip target, find elbow angle |

Forward kinematics is a well-posed mapping, whereas inverse kinematics is generally solved by geometric decoupling, algebraic manipulation, or iterative numerical methods. **(1 m for concluding sentence.)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a Jacobian singularity in a robotic manipulator? Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**
A Jacobian singularity is a configuration $\mathbf{q}_s$ at which the manipulator's Jacobian matrix $J(\mathbf{q}_s)$ loses full rank, i.e., $\det(J(\mathbf{q}_s)) = 0$. **(2 m)**

*Example:* For a 2-link planar arm with $l_1, l_2 > 0$, the determinant of the Jacobian is $l_1 l_2 \sin(q_2)$. The manipulator is singular when $q_2 = 0°$ (arm fully extended) or $q_2 = 180°$ (arm folded back on itself), at which point the end-effector cannot move in the radial direction. **(1 m)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematics of a 2-link planar manipulator with link lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$ and joint angles $q_1, q_2$ using the homogeneous transformation matrix approach. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hence obtain the Jacobian matrix and identify the singular configurations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 m):** State the DH parameters.

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $l_1$ | 0 | 0 | $q_1$ |
| 2 | $l_2$ | 0 | 0 | $q_2$ |

**Step 2 (2 m):** Compute individual link transforms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0^1 &= \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & l_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & l_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\
T_1^2 &= \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & l_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & l_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (3 m):** Multiply to obtain $T_0^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0^2 &= T_0^1 \, T_1^2 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} c_{12} & -s_{12} & 0 & l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} \\ s_{12} & \phantom{-}c_{12} & 0 & l_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 m):** Extract the position vector.

$$\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} \\ l_1 s_1 + l_2 s_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (3 m):** Differentiate to find the Jacobian.

$$J(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} & -l_2 s_{12} \\ \phantom{-}l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} & \phantom{-}l_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (2 m):** Compute the determinant.

$$\det(J) = l_1 l_2 \sin(q_2)$$

**Step 3 (2 m):** Identify singular configurations.

Setting $\det(J) = 0 \Rightarrow q_2 = 0°$ (arm fully extended) or $q_2 = \pm 180°$ (arm folded back). At these configurations, the manipulator loses one Cartesian degree of freedom — it cannot move in the direction along the arm. Joint velocities blow up for any finite Cartesian velocity, which is the practical hazard.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** State the Euler–Lagrange equation of motion for an $n$-DOF robot manipulator. Identify each term physically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 1-DOF rotational joint with moment of inertia $I$, viscous friction coefficient $b$, and a torque $\tau$ applied, derive the equation of motion using Newton's second law. Then generalise the result to a 2-DOF arm whose joints are coupled by a non-linear spring of stiffness $k(q_1 - q_2)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

The canonical equation is:

$$M(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} + G(\mathbf{q}) + F(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$$

**Term-by-term identification (1 m each):**

- $M(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — **Mass (inertia) matrix**; symmetric, positive-definite; encodes the kinetic energy of the system.
- $C(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — **Coriolis and centrifugal vector**; accounts for velocity-dependent forces arising from rotating reference frames.
- $G(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — **Gravitational torque vector**; depends only on configuration.
- $F(\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — **Friction term** (viscous, Coulomb, static).
- $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — **Generalised actuator torques/forces** applied at the joints.

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (2 m):** Newton's second law for a single rotating link.

$$I \ddot{q} = \sum \tau_{\text{applied}} - b \dot{q}$$

$$\boxed{I \ddot{q} + b \dot{q} = \tau}$$

**Step 2 (2 m):** Extend to two coupled joints with non-linear coupling spring $k(q_1 - q_2)$. The spring exerts torque $+k(q_1 - q_2)$ on joint 1 and $-k(q_1 - q_2)$ on joint 2.

**Step 3 (2 m):** Write the coupled equations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 \ddot{q}_1 + b_1 \dot{q}_1 + k(q_1 - q_2) &= \tau_1 \\
I_2 \ddot{q}_2 + b_2 \dot{q}_2 - k(q_1 - q_2) &= \tau_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 m):** Assemble in matrix form.

$$
\begin{bmatrix} I_1 & 0 \\ 0 & I_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \ddot{q}_1 \\ \ddot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b_1 & 0 \\ 0 & b_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} k & -k \\ -k & k \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matches the canonical form $M \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C \dot{\mathbf{q}} + G = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ with $C$ representing friction and $G$ representing the configuration-dependent coupling torque.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Before You Submit!):**
> 1. **Sign errors in DH parameters** — A single sign error in $T_0^1$ propagates through the entire chain. Cross-verify the position vector by the geometric (trigonometric) method.
> 2. **Forgetting the rotation matrix** — Many students write only the translational column of $T_0^n$ and skip $R_{0}^{n}$. In KTU, the **orientation** part carries 2 marks by itself in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 3. **Confusing $\omega$ and $\dot{\theta}$** — Angular velocity vector $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ and scalar joint rate $\dot{\theta}_i$ are related by $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \sum_i \dot{\theta}_i \, \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$, not equal to it.
> 4. **In dynamics, do not omit the Coriolis term** — Even when the manipulator is *moving*, $C \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is non-zero unless the configuration is straight. Examiners specifically look for this.
> 5. **Singularity vs. workspace boundary** — A singular configuration is *internal* to the workspace (loss of DOF); a workspace boundary is an *external* limit. Do not interchange the two in viva voce.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Kinematics** = motion geometry, no forces; **Dynamics** = motion + forces.
- ☐ **DOF** of a rigid body in 3D = 6; DOF of an $n$-joint robot = $n$ (revolute) or depends on joint types.
- ☐ **DH parameters** are exactly **4** per link: $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$.
- ☐ Forward kinematics is **unique**; inverse kinematics is **multi-solution / non-linear**.
- ☐ **Geometric Jacobian** $J(\mathbf{q})$ maps $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \rightarrow \dot{\mathbf{x}}$.
- ☐ **Singularity** occurs when $\det(J) = 0 \Rightarrow$ loss of DOF, infinite joint rates.
- ☐ **Statics duality** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^T \mathbf{F}$ (force/torque dual of velocity mapping).
- ☐ **Euler–Lagrange** dynamics: $M \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C \dot{\mathbf{q}} + G = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
- ☐ **Newton–Euler** recursion is the real-time alternative (O(n)) to Lagrangian (O(n³)).
- ☐ For 2-link arm: $\det(J) = l_1 l_2 \sin(q_2)$; singularities at $q_2 = 0°, 180°$.
- ☐ **Cubic polynomial trajectories** are the minimum-degree smooth interpolants.
- ☐ In CPS, kinematics + dynamics are the *physical models* embedded inside the *cyber* control loop.
- ☐ Always state the **assumptions** (planar/spatial, rigid links, no friction/gravity) at the start of a derivation.
- ☐ Always **draw the coordinate frame diagram** before assigning DH parameters — KTU awards marks for this.
- ☐ Remember to use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (never `\|`) when writing determinants in your answer sheet.
- ☐ For the **manipulability index** $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^{T})}$, $w = 0$ indicates a singularity and $w_{\max}$ indicates the most dexterous configuration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCPT404 - INTRODUCTION TO PROGRAMMING FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensor Integration in Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Sensor Integration in Robotics

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor Integration in Robotics** is the systematic process of combining, calibrating, and fusing data from multiple heterogeneous sensors (proprioceptive and exteroceptive) to produce a coherent, accurate, and reliable representation of a robot's internal state and its external environment. This forms the perception backbone of any Cyber-Physical System (CPS) operating in the physical world.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, sensor integration is not merely about connecting a sensor to a microcontroller. It encompasses:
- **Hardware interfacing** (signal conditioning, ADC, communication protocols)
- **Data synchronization** (time-stamping, sampling-rate matching)
- **Sensor fusion** (combining noisy multi-sensor data using statistical estimators)
- **Actuation feedback** (closing the loop with controllers)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Senses:**
> Think of a robot as a person walking into a dark, crowded room. The person does not rely on just **one** sense. They use **eyes** (vision/camera), **ears** (audio/microphone), **skin** (touch/tactile), and an internal sense of **balance** (IMU/gyroscope). The brain continuously merges all these streams to answer: *Where am I? What is around me? How am I moving?* Sensor integration is the engineering equivalent of this **biological sensory fusion**.

### 1.3 Key Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | $R$ | Sensor-dependent | Maximum/minimum measurable quantity |
| **Accuracy** | $\mathcal{A}$ | $\pm 0.1\%$ to $\pm 5\%$ | Closeness to true value |
| **Precision** | $\mathcal{P}$ | Variance / std. deviation | Repeatability of measurement |
| **Resolution** | $\Delta x$ | Sensor-dependent | Smallest detectable change |
| **Bandwidth** | $f_{bw}$ | **Hz** | Max sampling frequency |
| **Latency** | $\tau_{lat}$ | ms | Time delay of measurement |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | SNR | **dB** | $\mathbf{10 \log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners consistently differentiate between **Accuracy** and **Precision**. Accuracy = closeness to truth; Precision = closeness of repeated measurements to each other. A sensor can be *precise but inaccurate* (systematic bias) or *accurate on average but imprecise* (high random noise).

### 1.4 Classification of Robotic Sensors

Robotic sensors are broadly classified into two families:

**A. Proprioceptive Sensors** — measure the robot's *internal* state.
- Encoders (wheel/joint position), **IMU** (Inertial Measurement Unit), current sensors, force/torque sensors at joints.

**B. Exteroceptive Sensors** — measure the *external* environment.
- **LIDAR**, cameras (RGB/Depth), ultrasonic sensors, GPS, tactile/contact sensors.

A second orthogonal classification:
- **Active sensors**: emit energy (LIDAR, sonar, IR).
- **Passive sensors**: only receive (camera, encoder, accelerometer).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sensor Integration Pipeline (Block-Level View)

The integration of sensors in a robotic CPS follows a canonical five-stage pipeline:

1. **Signal Acquisition** — raw analog/digital data is captured from the transducer.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — amplification, filtering (e.g., low-pass to remove high-frequency noise), and linearization.
3. **Digitization** — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) using an N-bit quantizer:
$$V_{digital} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^{N} \right\rfloor$$
where $N$ is the ADC resolution (e.g., $N = 10 \rightarrow 1024$ levels) and $V_{ref}$ is the reference voltage.
4. **Calibration & Compensation** — removal of bias ($b$), scale-factor error ($s$), and non-linearity using a calibration curve.
5. **Sensor Fusion & State Estimation** — combining multiple sensor streams using estimators such as the **Kalman Filter**, **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**, or **Particle Filter**.

## 2.2 Why Integration? The Need for Sensor Fusion

> [!NOTE]
> **Why one sensor is never enough:**
> Every real sensor is corrupted by **noise**, has limited **range**, and suffers from **drift**. A single sensor cannot simultaneously provide high accuracy, high bandwidth, and robustness. Sensor fusion mathematically *exploits the complementary strengths* of multiple sensors while suppressing their individual weaknesses.

For example:
- **IMU** gives high-frequency motion data but **drifts** over time.
- **GPS** gives absolute position but updates at only **1–10 Hz** with multi-meter noise.
- **Fusing IMU + GPS** yields a system that is both **high-bandwidth** and **drift-free**.

## 2.3 Sensor Noise Modeling

Every sensor measurement $z(t)$ of a true quantity $x(t)$ is modeled as:

$$z(t) = h\!\left(x(t)\right) + b + \eta(t)$$

where:
- $h(\cdot)$ is the sensor's transfer function (often linear: $h(x) = s \cdot x$)
- $b$ is the **bias** (deterministic offset)
- $\eta(t)$ is the **measurement noise** (typically modeled as zero-mean Gaussian: $\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$)

The **standard deviation** $\sigma$ defines the sensor's noise floor. Two standard noise models are:

**Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN):** the noise power is flat across all frequencies.

**Bias-Stable Noise (Allan Variance):** for inertial sensors, the noise is decomposed into:
$$\sigma_{total}^{2} = \sigma_{QN}^{2} + \sigma_{ARW}^{2} + \sigma_{BI}^{2} + \sigma_{RRW}^{2} + \sigma_{RW}^{2}$$

representing quantisation, angle/m Velocity random walk, bias instability, rate random walk, and rate ramp respectively.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Key Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC Quantization | $V_{d} = \lfloor V_{in}/V_{ref} \cdot 2^{N} \rfloor$ | $N$ = bits, $V_{ref}$ = Volts |
| 2 | Quantization step (LSB) | $\Delta = V_{ref}/2^{N}$ | Volts |
| 3 | Sensor Model | $z = h(x) + b + \eta$ | $\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$ |
| 4 | SNR | $\mathrm{SNR} = 10\log_{10}(P_{s}/P_{n})$ | **dB** |
| 5 | Kalman Predict (state) | $\hat{x}^{-}_{k} = A\hat{x}^{+}_{k-1} + Bu_{k-1}$ | $\hat{x}$ = state estimate |
| 6 | Kalman Predict (covariance) | $P^{-}_{k} = AP^{+}_{k-1}A^{T} + Q$ | $Q$ = process noise covariance |
| 7 | Kalman Gain | $K_{k} = P^{-}_{k}H^{T}(HP^{-}_{k}H^{T}+R)^{-1}$ | $R$ = measurement noise covariance |
| 8 | Kalman Update (state) | $\hat{x}^{+}_{k} = \hat{x}^{-}_{k} + K_{k}(z_{k} - H\hat{x}^{-}_{k})$ | $z_{k}$ = measurement |
| 9 | Kalman Update (covariance) | $P^{+}_{k} = (I - K_{k}H)P^{-}_{k}$ | Symmetric, positive-definite |
| 10 | Coordinate Rotation (2D) | $\begin{bmatrix}x'\\y'\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x\\y\end{bmatrix}$ | $\theta$ = rotation angle (**rad**) |
| 11 | LIDAR Range | $r = (c \cdot \Delta t)/2$ | $c$ = speed of light, $\Delta t$ = time-of-flight |
| 12 | Accelerometer (static) | $a_{measured} = g\sin\theta$ | tilt $\theta$ from gravity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Kalman Filter equations (rows 5–9) are the **single most important formula group** for KTU board questions on sensor fusion. Memorize the **predict-then-update** structure and the role of $Q$ (process) vs $R$ (measurement) covariance matrices.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Vehicles (Mobileye, Waymo, Tesla Autopilot):** fuse camera + radar + LIDAR + GPS + IMU.
- **Aerial Drones (PX4, ArduPilot):** IMU + barometer + GPS + magnetometer for stable flight.
- **Industrial Manipulators (KUKA, FANUC):** joint encoders + force/torque sensors for compliant assembly.
- **Medical Robotics (Da Vinci):** vision + haptic feedback for tele-surgery.
- **Humanoid Robots (ASIMO, Atlas):** IMU + foot pressure + vision for balance and locomotion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — ADC Quantization Step Size

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the relationship between ADC resolution $N$, reference voltage $V_{ref}$, and the smallest measurable voltage change (Least Significant Bit, LSB).

**Step 1 — Define the analog input range.**
The ADC can map input voltages from $0$ to $V_{ref}$ (unipolar case). The total analog range is therefore:

$$
V_{range} = V_{ref} - 0 = V_{ref}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the digital range.**
An $N$-bit ADC can represent $2^{N}$ distinct digital codes (from $0$ to $2^{N}-1$). The total number of discrete steps is:

$$
N_{steps} = 2^{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the size of one step (LSB).**
By definition, the LSB is the analog voltage corresponding to one increment of the digital code:

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{range}}{N_{steps}} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N}}
$$

**Step 4 — Quantization error bound.**
The maximum quantization error is half of one LSB:

$$
e_{q} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N+1}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-favourite):**
For a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\,\mathrm{V}$:

$$
\Delta = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} \approx 0.806\;\mathrm{mV}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the resolution bits ($N \to N+1$) **halves** $\Delta$ and therefore doubles the measurement precision. This is the *physical basis* of why medical and aerospace CPS use 16-bit or 24-bit ADCs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — The Kalman Filter Predict Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Starting from a generic linear time-invariant (LTI) state-space model, derive the Kalman prediction equations for the a-priori state and covariance.

**Step 1 — State the discrete-time system model.**
A linear robot state evolves as:

$$
x_{k} = A\,x_{k-1} + B\,u_{k-1} + w_{k-1}
$$

where $w_{k-1} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ is process noise with covariance $Q$.

**Step 2 — State the measurement model.**

$$
z_{k} = H\,x_{k} + v_{k}, \qquad v_{k} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)
$$

**Step 3 — Take the conditional expectation of both sides of the state equation** (using the fact that $\mathbb{E}[w_{k-1}] = 0$):

$$
\mathbb{E}[x_{k} \mid z_{1:k-1}] = A\,\mathbb{E}[x_{k-1} \mid z_{1:k-1}] + B\,u_{k-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the posterior estimate notation** $\hat{x}^{+}_{k-1}$ for the previous filtered estimate and $\hat{x}^{-}_{k}$ for the new prediction:

$$
\hat{x}^{-}_{k} = A\,\hat{x}^{+}_{k-1} + B\,u_{k-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the a-priori covariance.** Define the prediction error $e^{-}_{k} = x_{k} - \hat{x}^{-}_{k}$. Substituting the system model and prediction:

$$
e^{-}_{k} = A(x_{k-1} - \hat{x}^{+}_{k-1}) + w_{k-1} = A\,e^{+}_{k-1} + w_{k-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the covariance** $P^{-}_{k} = \mathbb{E}[e^{-}_{k}(e^{-}_{k})^{T}]$:

$$
P^{-}_{k} = \mathbb{E}\left[(A\,e^{+}_{k-1} + w_{k-1})(A\,e^{+}_{k-1} + w_{k-1})^{T}\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Expand the product.** Since $e^{+}_{k-1}$ and $w_{k-1}$ are uncorrelated (cross-term is zero), and $\mathbb{E}[w_{k-1}w_{k-1}^{T}] = Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P^{-}_{k} &= A\,\mathbb{E}[e^{+}_{k-1}(e^{+}_{k-1})^{T}]\,A^{T} + \mathbb{E}[w_{k-1}w_{k-1}^{T}] \\
&= A\,P^{+}_{k-1}\,A^{T} + Q
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the **prediction step** of the Kalman Filter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Kalman Gain and Update Step

**Step 1 — Define the innovation (measurement residual).**

$$
\tilde{y}_{k} = z_{k} - H\,\hat{x}^{-}_{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the updated estimate as a correction of the prediction** weighted by the Kalman gain $K_{k}$:

$$
\hat{x}^{+}_{k} = \hat{x}^{-}_{k} + K_{k}\,\tilde{y}_{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive $K_{k}$ by minimising the a-posteriori covariance trace** $\mathrm{tr}(P^{+}_{k})$. Set $\partial \mathrm{tr}(P^{+}_{k}) / \partial K_{k} = 0$. After algebraic manipulation (substitute the error, expand, differentiate):

$$
K_{k} = P^{-}_{k}\,H^{T}\,(H\,P^{-}_{k}\,H^{T} + R)^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Final updated covariance** (the "Joseph form" simplification):

$$
P^{+}_{k} = (I - K_{k}\,H)\,P^{-}_{k}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive meaning of $K_{k}$:**
> - If $R \to 0$ (perfect sensor): $K_{k} \to H^{-1}$ → **trust the measurement completely**.
> - If $P^{-}_{k} \to 0$ (perfect prediction): $K_{k} \to 0$ → **ignore the measurement**.
> - $K_{k}$ is essentially the *optimal adaptive blending factor* between model and sensor.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — LIDAR Time-of-Flight Range

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the distance formula for a pulsed LIDAR sensor.

A LIDAR emits a laser pulse that travels to a target and reflects back. The round-trip time $\Delta t$ is measured. With the speed of light $c$:

$$
r = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

The factor of **2** accounts for the round trip (down and back). For a typical automotive LIDAR with $r = 100\,\mathrm{m}$:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{2r}{c} = \frac{2 \cdot 100}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 0.667\;\mu\mathrm{s}
$$

This sub-microsecond timing requires high-precision timers, motivating the use of **TCSPC** (Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting) hardware in CPS.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Robotic Sensors

| Sensor | Type | Measures | Range | Bandwidth | Noise / Drift | Best Fused With |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoder (optical)** | Proprioceptive, Active | Joint/wheel position | Unlimited (counting) | kHz | Negligible | IMU, force sensor |
| **IMU (6-axis)** | Proprioceptive, Passive | Accel + Angular rate | $\pm 16g$, $\pm 2000\,^\circ/s$ | $\geq 1\,\mathrm{kHz}$ | Bias drift ~$\mu$g | GPS, magnetometer |
| **LIDAR** | Exteroceptive, Active | Range to target | $0.1 - 200\,\mathrm{m}$ | $10 - 100\,\mathrm{Hz}$ | $\sigma_{r} \approx 2-3\,\mathrm{cm}$ | Camera, IMU |
| **Camera (RGB-D)** | Exteroceptive, Passive | Color + Depth (m) | $0.3 - 10\,\mathrm{m}$ | $30 - 60\,\mathrm{fps}$ | Lighting dependent | LIDAR, IMU |
| **GPS** | Exteroceptive, Passive | Global position | Global | $1 - 10\,\mathrm{Hz}$ | $\sigma \approx 2-5\,\mathrm{m}$ | IMU, barometer |
| **Ultrasonic** | Exteroceptive, Active | Range | $0.02 - 10\,\mathrm{m}$ | $40\,\mathrm{Hz}$ | Beam divergence | IR, encoder |
| **Force/Torque** | Proprioceptive | Force at end-effector | $\pm$ hundreds of N | $1\,\mathrm{kHz}$ | Hysteresis | Encoder, vision |
| **Magnetometer** | Proprioceptive | Heading | $360^\circ$ | $100\,\mathrm{Hz}$ | Magnetic interference | IMU, GPS |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Do not confuse **proprioceptive** (internal state — "where are my joints?") with **exteroceptive** (external world — "what is in front of me?"). Mixing them up costs full marks in definition questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sensor Integration Architecture in a Robotic CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV["External Environment"] -->|Stimulus| SENS1["LIDAR Sensor"]
    ENV -->|Light| SENS2["RGB-D Camera"]
    ENV -->|RF| SENS3["GPS Receiver"]
    ROB["Robot Body / Joints"] -->|Motion| SENS4["IMU 6-Axis"]
    ROB -->|Angle| SENS5["Optical Encoder"]
    ROB -->|Force| SENS6["Force Torque Sensor"]

    SENS1 --> COND1["Signal Conditioning\n+ ADC"]
    SENS2 --> COND2["Image Processing\n+ ADC"]
    SENS3 --> COND3["RF Front End\n+ Decoder"]
    SENS4 --> COND4["Analog Filter\n+ ADC"]
    SENS5 --> COND5["Quadrature Decoder"]
    SENS6 --> COND6["Strain Gauge Amp\n+ ADC"]

    COND1 --> FUSE["Central Sensor\nFusion Engine\nKalman / EKF / PF"]
    COND2 --> FUSE
    COND3 --> FUSE
    COND4 --> FUSE
    COND5 --> FUSE
    COND6 --> FUSE

    FUSE -->|State Estimate| CTRL["Robot Motion\nController\nPID / MPC"]
    CTRL --> ACT["Motor Drivers\nand Actuators"]
    ACT -->|Physical Motion| ROB
    CTRL -->|Telemetry| UI["Operator\nHMI / Cloud"]
    FUSE -->|Logged Data| UI

    style FUSE fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CTRL fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ACT fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style ENV fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style ROB fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *environment* and *robot body* are the two physical domains that produce stimuli. These are sensed in parallel by **six heterogeneous sensors**, each passing through its own conditioning stage before converging at the **central fusion engine** (highlighted in yellow). The fused state estimate feeds the **controller**, which commands **actuators**, closing the cyber-physical loop.

## 4.2 Kalman Filter Recursive Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT["Initialize\nx_hat_0, P_0"] --> PRED1["Predict State\nx_hat_minus = A x_hat_plus + B u"]
    PRED1 --> PRED2["Predict Covariance\nP_minus = A P_plus A_T + Q"]
    PRED2 --> MEAS["Receive New\nMeasurement z_k"]
    MEAS --> GAIN["Compute Kalman Gain\nK = P_minus H_T inv H P_minus H_T + R"]
    GAIN --> UPD1["Update State\nx_hat_plus = x_hat_minus + K z_k - H x_hat_minus"]
    UPD1 --> UPD2["Update Covariance\nP_plus = I - K H P_minus"]
    UPD2 --> PRED1

    style INIT fill:#90e0ef,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style PRED1 fill:#caf0f8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style PRED2 fill:#caf0f8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style MEAS fill:#ffafcc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style GAIN fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style UPD1 fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style UPD2 fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Kalman Filter is a **two-phase recursive loop**: a **time-update (predict)** in blue, and a **measurement-update (correct)** in pink/yellow. This predict-correct cycle repeats every time a new measurement arrives.

## 4.3 Sensor Hierarchy in Mobile Robotics (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Layer 1: Low-level Raw Sensing\nEncoder pulses, ADC samples"]
    L2["Layer 2: Pre-processing\nFiltering, calibration, time-stamping"]
    L3["Layer 3: Feature Extraction\nEdge detection, corner detection, landmark matching"]
    L4["Layer 4: Sensor Fusion\nKalman / Particle Filter"]
    L5["Layer 5: State Estimation\nPosition, velocity, orientation, map"]
    L6["Layer 6: Decision and Control\nPath planning, obstacle avoidance"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6

    style L1 fill:#e63946,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#f1a208,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L3 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L4 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style L5 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style L6 fill:#7209b7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** mark pattern: Part A = 3 marks (short answer), Part B = 14 marks (with internal choice).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors in robotics. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Proprioceptive sensors measure the **internal state** of the robot (joint angles, wheel speed, battery voltage), while exteroceptive sensors measure the **external environment** (distance to obstacles, ambient light, GPS coordinates).
- Proprioceptive examples (1+1 = 2): (i) Optical encoder on a joint, (ii) IMU accelerometer.
- Exteroceptive examples (1): (i) LIDAR, (ii) RGB camera.

*(Full marks require the distinction statement + 2 examples of each.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
**What is sensor fusion? Why is a single sensor insufficient for robust robotic perception?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Sensor fusion** is the algorithmic combination of data from multiple sensors to produce a more accurate, reliable, and complete estimate than any individual sensor can provide (1 mark).
A single sensor is insufficient because (1+1 marks):
1. Every sensor has **noise** and **drift**; no single sensor is perfectly reliable.
2. Each sensor has limited **range** and **bandwidth**; one modality cannot cover all operating conditions (e.g., a camera fails in darkness; GPS fails indoors).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 scheme, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sensor Modeling and ADC Design

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the quantization step size (LSB) of an N-bit ADC. A robotic CPS uses a **12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\,\mathrm{V}$** to read a strain-gauge signal ranging from 0.2 V to 2.8 V. Calculate the LSB voltage and the number of digital codes spanned by the signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Develop the general linear sensor measurement model $z = h(x) + b + \eta$. For a LIDAR with range $r = 50\,\mathrm{m}$ and measurement noise standard deviation $\sigma = 0.03\,\mathrm{m}$, compute the **SNR in dB** and the 3$\sigma$ confidence interval of the reading. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):**

Starting from the definition of LSB as the ratio of analog range to digital steps:

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{N}} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096}
$$

Computing the numerical value:

$$
\Delta \approx 0.806 \times 10^{-3}\,\mathrm{V} = 0.806\,\mathrm{mV}
$$

(3 marks for derivation + calculation)

Signal span: $\Delta V = 2.8 - 0.2 = 2.6\,\mathrm{V}$.

Number of codes spanned:

$$
N_{codes} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta} = \frac{2.6}{0.000806} \approx 3226
$$

This is within the 12-bit maximum of **4096 codes**, so the ADC is correctly sized (2 marks for the calculation, 2 marks for the engineering conclusion).

**(b) Sensor Model and SNR (7 marks):**

The general linear sensor model:

$$
z = s \cdot x + b + \eta, \qquad \eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})
$$

For the LIDAR with $r = 50\,\mathrm{m}$, $s = 1$, $b = 0$:

$$
P_{signal} \propto r^{2} = 50^{2} = 2500
$$

$$
P_{noise} \propto \sigma^{2} = (0.03)^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-4}
$$

SNR in dB:

$$
\mathrm{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2500}{9 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(2.778 \times 10^{6})
$$

$$
\mathrm{SNR} \approx 64.4\,\mathrm{dB}
$$

(3 marks for the model, 2 marks for SNR, 2 marks for the confidence interval)

3$\sigma$ confidence interval: $r \in [50 - 0.09,\; 50 + 0.09] = [49.91,\; 50.09]\,\mathrm{m}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Kalman Filter for Sensor Fusion

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **need for sensor fusion** in robotics with a real-world example (e.g., IMU + GPS for a drone). State the **advantages** of using a Kalman Filter as the fusion algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 1-D position-tracking robot, the state is $x_{k} = [p_{k},\; v_{k}]^{T}$. The system matrices are:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & \Delta t\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix}0\\ \Delta t\end{bmatrix}, \quad H = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\end{bmatrix}
$$

with $\Delta t = 0.1\,\mathrm{s}$, $Q = \mathrm{diag}(0.01, 0.001)$, $R = [0.5]$. Given $P^{+}_{k-1} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0\\ 0 & 0.2\end{bmatrix}$, compute the **predicted covariance $P^{-}_{k}$**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Need for Sensor Fusion and Kalman Filter (7 marks):**

**Why fusion is needed (4 marks):**
- IMU provides **high-rate** (1 kHz) acceleration/angular velocity but suffers from **integration drift**.
- GPS provides **absolute, drift-free position** but at low rate (1–10 Hz) and is noisy.
- Fusing both yields: high bandwidth + drift-free + reduced noise = a single best estimate.

**Kalman Filter advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Optimal** in the minimum mean-square-error (MMSE) sense for linear Gaussian systems.
2. **Recursive** — requires only the previous estimate, not the entire history (memory efficient for embedded CPS).
3. Provides **uncertainty quantification** (covariance $P$) alongside the state estimate, which is critical for safety in autonomous CPS.

**(b) Predicted Covariance Calculation (7 marks):**

Using $P^{-}_{k} = A\,P^{+}_{k-1}\,A^{T} + Q$:

**Step 1 — Compute $A\,P^{+}_{k-1}$:**

$$
A\,P^{+}_{k-1} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.1\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0\\ 0 & 0.2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.02\\ 0 & 0.2\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(A\,P^{+}_{k-1})\,A^{T}$:**

Note that $A^{T} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\\ 0.1 & 1\end{bmatrix}$. Then:

$$
(A\,P^{+}_{k-1})\,A^{T} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5 & 0.02\\ 0 & 0.2\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0\\ 0.1 & 1\end{bmatrix}
$$

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Entry (1,1)} &= 0.5 \cdot 1 + 0.02 \cdot 0.1 = 0.502 \\
\text{Entry (1,2)} &= 0.5 \cdot 0 + 0.02 \cdot 1 = 0.02 \\
\text{Entry (2,1)} &= 0 \cdot 1 + 0.2 \cdot 0.1 = 0.02 \\
\text{Entry (2,2)} &= 0 \cdot 0 + 0.2 \cdot 1 = 0.2
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$
A\,P^{+}_{k-1}\,A^{T} = \begin{bmatrix}0.502 & 0.02\\ 0.02 & 0.2\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Add process noise covariance $Q$:**

$$
P^{-}_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}0.502 & 0.02\\ 0.02 & 0.2\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.01 & 0\\ 0 & 0.001\end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
P^{-}_{k} = \begin{bmatrix}0.512 & 0.02\\ 0.02 & 0.201\end{bmatrix}
$$

This positive-definite symmetric matrix is the a-priori covariance, ready for the next measurement update.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in LIDAR time-of-flight** — students often write $r = c \cdot \Delta t$ instead of $r = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$. Full 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Confusing predict and update** — the predict step uses $Q$, the update step uses $R$. Swapping them is a fatal conceptual error.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit in long answers.
> 4. **Mixing proprioceptive and exteroceptive** — these terms are *not* interchangeable; misuse loses 1–2 marks in Part A.
> 5. **Not verifying positive-definiteness of $P$** — a correct Kalman result is always symmetric and positive-definite. If your answer is not, you have made a matrix arithmetic error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Sensor Integration in Robotics:**

- **Definition:** Sensor integration = combining multiple sensors + signal conditioning + fusion into a coherent perception system.
- **Two sensor families:** Proprioceptive (internal) vs Exteroceptive (external). **Active vs Passive** is an orthogonal classification.
- **Five-stage pipeline:** Acquisition → Conditioning → Digitization (ADC) → Calibration → Fusion.
- **ADC key formula:** $\Delta = V_{ref}/2^{N}$; quantization error $\pm \Delta/2$.
- **Generic sensor model:** $z = h(x) + b + \eta$, with $\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$.
- **SNR formula:** $\mathrm{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{s}/P_{n})$ in **dB**.
- **Kalman Filter is the workhorse fusion algorithm** — predict using $A, B, Q$; update using $H, R$.
- **Predict equations:** $\hat{x}^{-}_{k} = A\hat{x}^{+}_{k-1} + Bu_{k-1}$ and $P^{-}_{k} = AP^{+}_{k-1}A^{T} + Q$.
- **Update equations:** $K_{k} = P^{-}_{k}H^{T}(HP^{-}_{k}H^{T} + R)^{-1}$ and $\hat{x}^{+}_{k} = \hat{x}^{-}_{k} + K_{k}(z_{k} - H\hat{x}^{-}_{k})$.
- **LIDAR range formula:** $r = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$ (round-trip, so divide by 2).
- **Kalman Gain intuition:** Large $K$ → trust the sensor; small $K$ → trust the model.
- **CPS Loop:** Sensors → Fusion Engine → Controller → Actuators → Physical World → (back to Sensors).
- **Common exam trap:** Accuracy ≠ Precision. Accuracy is closeness to truth; precision is repeatability.
- **Engineering applications to memorize:** Autonomous vehicles, drones, manipulators, medical robots, humanoids.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading Sensor Data and Interfacing with Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCPT404 - INTRODUCTION TO PROGRAMMING FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reading Sensor Data and Interfacing with Hardware # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)**, *Reading Sensor Data and Interfacing with Hardware* refers to the systematic process of **acquiring physical-world measurements** through transducers (sensors), **conditioning and converting** the resulting analog/digital signals into a machine-readable form, and **exchanging** this data between the physical layer (sensors, actuators) and the computational layer (microcontroller, embedded processor) through well-defined communication protocols. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCPT404)** syllabus, this topic covers: - **Sensor data acquisition** — sampling, quantization, and digitization of physical signals. - **Hardware interfacing paradigms** — GPIO, ADC, I²C, SPI, UART. - **Signal conditioning** — amplification, filtering, and linearization. - **Embedded programming constructs** — register-level access, polling, interrupts. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):** > This topic directly maps to **CO3 — *Interface physical sensors and actuators with computational units using standard embedded protocols.*** It is a high-weightage module in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator at a Diplomatic Summit" Imagine a **diplomatic summit** where two people speak completely different languages: | Role in Analogy | CPS Equivalent | Function | |---|---|---| | Foreign dignitary speaking *French* | **Physical Sensor** (e.g., LM35 temperature sensor) | Produces a *physical phenomenon* (voltage) | | **Translator** standing in between | **ADC + Microcontroller Firmware** | Converts *physical signals* into *digital numbers* | | Local host speaking *English* | **CPU / Main Application** | Uses the *digital data* to make decisions | | Diplomatic protocol (handshake, agenda) | **I²C / SPI / UART** | Defines *rules of conversation* | Just as a translator must follow strict rules (grammar, timing, vocabulary) to faithfully convey meaning, an **embedded system** must follow strict **timing diagrams, voltage levels, and protocol handshakes** to faithfully read sensor data. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition — Sensor:** > A *sensor* is a transducer that converts a **physical quantity** (temperature, pressure, light, motion) into an **electrical signal** (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency). The inverse device is called an **actuator**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following metrics govern all sensor interfacing — they are **board-exam favorites**: - **ADC Resolution:** $n$ bits → $2^n$ discrete levels. - **Reference Voltage:** $V_{ref}$ (typically **3.3 V** or **5 V**). - **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. - **Standard I²C Clock Speeds:** **100 kHz** (Standard), **400 kHz** (Fast), **3.4 MHz** (High-Speed). - **Standard SPI Clock:** up to **10 MHz** (device-dependent). - **Logic Voltage Levels:** **TTL (5 V)** vs **CMOS (3.3 V / 1.8 V)**. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Sensor-to-CPU Data Pipeline (Six Logical Stages) Every sensor reading in a CPS traverses the following deterministic pipeline. Each stage is a **potential point of failure** and a **potential KTU exam question**. ### Stage 1 — Physical Phenomenon Detection The sensor's sensing element (e.g., a thermistor, photodiode, MEMS accelerometer) responds to a physical stimulus. The output is usually an **analog continuous signal**. ### Stage 2 — Signal Conditioning Raw sensor outputs are often weak, noisy, or non-linear. Conditioning includes: - **Amplification** — using op-amp circuits (e.g., *instrumentation amplifier* INA128). - **Filtering** — low-pass RC filter to remove high-frequency noise. - **Linearization** — applying a calibration curve $V_{out} = f(T)$. ### Stage 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) The **ADC** samples the conditioned analog voltage and assigns a discrete digital code. ### Stage 4 — Digital Protocol Exchange The digital code is transported to the CPU via: - **Parallel bus** (fast, pin-heavy). - **Serial bus** (I²C, SPI, UART) — common in modern CPS. ### Stage 5 — Driver / Firmware Processing A *device driver* unpacks raw bytes, applies calibration constants, and exposes a clean API to higher layers (e.g., `read_temperature()`). ### Stage 6 — Application / Control Decision The application consumes the data, applies control logic (PID, state machine), and commands **actuators**. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage > [!NOTE] > **Why can't we connect a sensor directly to a CPU?** > CPUs operate on **discrete digital logic levels** (0 V and $V_{DD}$). A raw sensor outputs a **continuous analog voltage** (e.g., 1.2437 V) which the CPU cannot interpret. An **ADC** is the mandatory bridge. > [!NOTE] > **Why serial protocols instead of parallel?** > Parallel buses require N data lines for N bits, consuming precious microcontroller pins. Serial protocols trade **speed for pin count**, ideal for resource-constrained embedded nodes (IoT, CPS). ## 2.3 ADC Resolution & Quantization (The Core Math) An $n$-bit ADC divides the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ into $2^n$ equal **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** intervals. $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$ The **quantization error** (maximum ±½ LSB) is: $$E_{q} = \pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Expression | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | ADC Step Size (LSB) | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | **Volts (V)** | Converts digital code to voltage | | 2 | Quantization Error | $E_{q} = \pm \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$ | **Volts (V)** | Bound on ADC precision | | 3 | Reconstructed Voltage | $V_{in} = D \cdot V_{LSB}$ | **Volts (V)** | Reads ADC code $D$ back as voltage | | 4 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | Minimum sample rate to avoid aliasing | | 5 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ | **dB** | Theoretical SNR of an ideal $n$-bit ADC | | 6 | Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta x}$ | **V/unit** | Output swing per unit of physical input | | 7 | I²C Bit-Rate | $f_{SCL}$ | **Hz** | Serial clock on SCL line | | 8 | Sensor Transfer Function (Linear) | $y = mx + c$ | **Physical unit** | Calibration of raw reading to physical value | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on `|x|` notation:** Throughout these notes, absolute values are written using $\lvert x \rvert$ to preserve markdown table integrity. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive ECUs:** Read crankshaft position via SPI from a Hall-effect sensor at 10 kHz. - **Medical CPS (pacemakers):** Read ECG via 12-bit ADC sampled at **500 Hz**. - **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors broadcast over **I²C** every 60 s via LoRa. - **Industrial IoT:** Vibration sensors use **±½ LSB quantization** to detect 0.01 g accelerometer drift. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Digital Code to Physical Voltage A 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ returns a digital code $D = 743$. Find the input voltage. ### Step 1 — Identify parameters - $n = 10$ bits, $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$, $D = 743$. ### Step 2 — Compute the LSB step size $$ V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} $$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate the fraction numerically $$ V_{LSB} = 0.0048828125 \text{ V} = 4.8828 \text{ mV} $$ ### Step 4 — Multiply digital code by step size $$ V_{in} = D \cdot V_{LSB} = 743 \cdot \frac{5}{1024} $$ ### Step 5 — Final calculation $$ V_{in} = \frac{743 \times 5}{1024} = \frac{3715}{1024} \approx 3.6279 \text{ V} $$ > **Result:** The physical input voltage is approximately **3.628 V** — well within the [0 V, 5 V] range. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensor Calibration (LM35 Temperature Sensor) The **LM35** outputs **10 mV per °C** linearly. Suppose a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ returns $D = 410$. Find the temperature. ### Step 1 — Convert digital code to voltage $$ V_{in} = \frac{410 \times 5}{1024} = \frac{2050}{1024} \approx 2.0020 \text{ V} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply LM35 transfer function The LM35 transfer function is $T(°C) = \dfrac{V_{in}}{10 \text{ mV}}$. $$ T = \frac{2.0020 \text{ V}}{0.010 \text{ V/°C}} = 200.2 \text{ °C} $$ ### Step 3 — Validate against sensor range LM35 is rated for **−55 °C to +150 °C** (basic grade). The reading **200.2 °C is out of range** — indicating a **sensor fault or ADC misconfiguration**. (Excellent exam question to test understanding.) ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nyquist Frequency for Vibration Sensor A rotating machine has a maximum vibration frequency $f_{max} = 2.5\text{ kHz}$. Determine the minimum sampling rate. ### Step 1 — Apply Nyquist theorem $$ f_s \geq 2 f_{max} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $$ f_s \geq 2 \times 2500 = 5000 \text{ Hz} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply engineering safety factor (×2.5) $$ f_{s,\text{design}} = 2.5 \times 5000 = 12.5 \text{ kHz} $$ > **Result:** Use an ADC sample rate of **at least 12.5 kHz** to capture 2.5 kHz vibration with margin. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) For a 12-bit ADC, compute the theoretical SQNR. ### Step 1 — Apply formula $$ SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $n = 12$ $$ SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB} $$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns | Question Pattern | Given | Required | Key Formula | |---|---|---|---| | Code → Voltage | $D$, $n$, $V_{ref}$ | $V_{in}$ | $V_{in} = D \cdot V_{ref}/2^n$ | | Voltage → Code | $V_{in}$, $n$, $V_{ref}$ | $D$ | $D = \lfloor V_{in} \cdot 2^n / V_{ref} \rfloor$ | | Resolution | $V_{ref}$, $n$ | Smallest detectable $\Delta V$ | $V_{ref}/2^n$ | | Sampling rate | $f_{max}$ | $f_s$ | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | | Sensor calibration | Sensitivity $S$, $D$ | Physical quantity | $x = (D \cdot V_{ref}) / (2^n \cdot S)$ | # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sensor-to-CPU Data Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World
Temperature Pressure Light Motion] --> B[Sensor
Transducer] B --> C[Signal Conditioner
Amplifier and Filter] C --> D[ADC
Analog to Digital Converter] D --> E[Microcontroller
Firmware Driver] E --> F[Application Layer
Control Logic PID] F --> G[Actuator
Motor Valve LED] G --> H[Physical World
Feedback Effect] I[I2C SPI UART Bus] -.protocol.-> E J[Reference Voltage Vref] -.supplies.-> D K[Clock Source] -.timing.-> D ``` ## 4.2 I²C Bus Communication Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Master as Microcontroller Master participant Slave as Sensor Slave Address 0x48 participant Bus as I2C Bus SDA and SCL Master->>Bus: START Condition SDA falls while SCL high Master->>Slave: Send Address Byte 7 bit plus R/W bit Slave->>Master: ACK Acknowledge bit low Master->>Slave: Send Register Pointer Slave->>Master: ACK Master->>Slave: Repeated START Master->>Slave: Send Address with Read Bit Slave->>Master: ACK Slave->>Master: Transmit Data Byte MSB first Master->>Slave: NACK Not Acknowledge Master->>Bus: STOP Condition SDA rises while SCL high ``` ## 4.3 SPI Master-Slave Interfacing (4-Wire) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MasterNode[Microcontroller Master] MOSI1[MOSI Pin] MISO1[MISO Pin] SCK1[SCK Pin] SS1[SS Chip Select] end subgraph SlaveA[Sensor A CS pin 10] MOSIA[MOSI] MISOA[MISO] SCKA[SCK] end subgraph SlaveB[Sensor B CS pin 11] MOSIB[MOSI] MISOB[MISO] SCKB[SCK] end MasterNode -->|Shared MOSI MISO SCK| SlaveA MasterNode -->|Shared MOSI MISO SCK| SlaveB SS1 -->|Active Low| SlaveA SS1 -->|Active Low| SlaveB ``` ## 4.4 ADC Sampling State Machine (Firmware Perspective) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> TriggerADC: Timer Tick or External IRQ TriggerADC --> WaitEOC: Start Conversion via ADCSRA WaitEOC --> ReadData: ADC Conversion Complete Flag Set ReadData --> Process: Read ADCH and ADCL Registers Process --> ApplyFilter: Moving Average or Median Filter ApplyFilter --> UpdateBuffer: Store in Circular Buffer UpdateBuffer --> Idle: Return ReadData --> ErrorHandler: Timeout After 1 ms ErrorHandler --> Idle: Log Fault and Reset ``` ## 4.5 Polling vs Interrupt — Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need to Read Sensor] --> Q1{CPU Has Other Tasks?} Q1 -->|No Real-time Constraint| P[Use Polling Loop] Q1 -->|Yes Time Critical| Q2{Sensor Event Frequency?} Q2 -->|Low under 10 Hz| INT1[Use External Interrupt] Q2 -->|High over 1 kHz| DMA[Use DMA with Circular Buffer] Q2 -->|Periodic Fixed Rate| TMR[Use Timer Interrupt] P --> End[Implementation] INT1 --> End DMA --> End TMR --> End ``` # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define an ADC and explain its resolution with an example.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is an electronic circuit that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code. **Resolution** of an $n$-bit ADC is the smallest change in input voltage it can detect, given by: $$ V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} $$ **Example:** For a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$, $V_{LSB} = 5 / 1024 \approx 4.88\text{ mV}$. (3 marks for definition + formula + example.) --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Differentiate between I²C and SPI communication protocols.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | I²C | SPI | |---|---|---| | Lines required | **2 (SDA, SCL)** | **4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS)** | | Speed | Up to 3.4 MHz | Up to 10+ MHz | | Addressing | **7/10-bit slave address** | Hardware chip-select pin | | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | **Full-duplex** | | Multi-master | Yes | No | (3 marks for at least 3 distinct, accurate differences.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]** **(a)** Explain the working of a **10-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** with a neat block diagram. Compute the digital output code when an input voltage of **2.7 V** is applied to a 10-bit ADC with **$V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete signal chain** for acquiring temperature data from an **LM35 sensor** in a CPS node. List the protocols used and justify the choice. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** **Working of SAR ADC (4 marks):** A SAR ADC uses a binary search algorithm. The internal DAC generates trial voltages, and a comparator tests whether the input is higher or lower. The register keeps the bits from MSB to LSB. After $n$ cycles, an $n$-bit digital code is produced. **Step-by-step computation (3 marks):** $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^n}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor $$ $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{2.7 \times 1024}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2764.8}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 552.96 \right\rfloor = 552 $$ $$ D = (552)_{10} = (1000101000)_2 $$ #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** The complete signal chain: 1. **Sensor (LM35)** — outputs 10 mV/°C analog voltage. 2. **Amplifier** — non-inverting op-amp (gain = 5) to use full ADC range. 3. **Low-pass RC filter** — removes 50 Hz mains noise. 4. **ADC** — 12-bit, 1 kS/s, SAR type. 5. **Microcontroller** — I²C master, reads ADC via I²C at 400 kHz. 6. **Application** — displays °C on OLED / transmits to cloud. **Protocol Justification (3 marks):** I²C is chosen because LM35 is on the same board (short distance), only two pins are needed (GPIO conservation), and 400 kHz speed is more than sufficient for slow temperature changes. SPI is overkill; UART is asynchronous and unsuited to a single-master single-slave sensor. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**. An audio CPS system has a maximum audible frequency of **20 kHz**. Determine the minimum sampling rate and explain what happens if this rate is violated. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe **polling, interrupts, and DMA** as three methods of reading sensor data in a microcontroller.
